findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a large cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-filled cleft extending through the full thickness of the cerebral hemisphere, communicating the subarachnoid space with the lateral ventricle. The cleft is distinctly lined by dysplastic, polymicrogyric gray matter.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the absence of the septum pellucidum, which is frequently seen in association with this condition. Additional neural migration abnormalities, such as gray matter heterotopia, may also be present elsewhere in the brain.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of gliotic white matter lining the cleft with surrounding T2 hyperintensity. Furthermore, the lesion is not classically wedge-shaped and does not strictly conform to an arterial vascular territory.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the fact that the CSF-filled cortical cleft is lined by gray matter rather than gliotic white matter.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Schizencephaly (type II, open lip), given the full-thickness cortical cleft lined by polymicrogyric gray matter. This represents a congenital neural migration anomaly resulting from an intrauterine insult, and when the cleft edges are separated and filled with CSF, it is classified as the open-lip variant.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Prior infarct with encephalomalacia, however this would result in a CSF-filled cavity lined by gliotic white matter rather than dysplastic gray matter. Additionally, chronic infarction often has a wedge-shaped morphology and demonstrates surrounding parenchymal T2 hyperintensity due to gliosis.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Porencephalic cyst, given it also presents as a CSF-filled parenchymal cavity communicating with the ventricle, and misclassifying it could mean missing broader congenital neural migration anomalies. However, porencephalic cysts result from a perinatal destructive insult and are classically lined by gliotic white matter, not gray matter.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to precisely characterize the extent of the defect, confirm the presence of gray matter lining, and evaluate for associated midline anomalies such as septo-optic dysplasia.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to establish the congenital stability of the lesion and to ensure there are no new complications such as evolving hydrocephalus.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for definitively demonstrating whether the cleft is lined by gray or white matter and for identifying subtle associated cortical malformations.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted sequences to exquisitely differentiate gray from white matter lining the cleft, alongside T2 and FLAIR sequences to evaluate for any surrounding gliosis which would point towards a destructive encephalomalacic etiology.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Neurology MDT. My report would clearly document the presence of the gray matter-lined cleft, whether it is unilateral or bilateral, and the status of the septum pellucidum and optic nerves to guide developmental support and seizure management.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a large cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-filled cleft extending through the full thickness of the cerebral hemisphere, communicating the subarachnoid space with the lateral ventricle. The cleft is distinctly lined by dysplastic, polymicrogyric gray matter. Supporting features include the absence of the septum pellucidum, which is frequently seen in association with this condition. Additional neural migration abnormalities, such as gray matter heterotopia, may also be present elsewhere in the brain. Importantly, there is no evidence of gliotic white matter lining the cleft with surrounding T2 hyperintensity. Furthermore, the lesion is not classically wedge-shaped and does not strictly conform to an arterial vascular territory. The feature that most influences my thinking is the fact that the CSF-filled cortical cleft is lined by gray matter rather than gliotic white matter.

The appearances are most in keeping with Schizencephaly (type II, open lip), given the full-thickness cortical cleft lined by polymicrogyric gray matter. This represents a congenital neural migration anomaly resulting from an intrauterine insult, and when the cleft edges are separated and filled with CSF, it is classified as the open-lip variant. The principle differential would be Prior infarct with encephalomalacia, however this would result in a CSF-filled cavity lined by gliotic white matter rather than dysplastic gray matter. Additionally, chronic infarction often has a wedge-shaped morphology and demonstrates surrounding parenchymal T2 hyperintensity due to gliosis. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Porencephalic cyst, given it also presents as a CSF-filled parenchymal cavity communicating with the ventricle, and misclassifying it could mean missing broader congenital neural migration anomalies. However, porencephalic cysts result from a perinatal destructive insult and are classically lined by gliotic white matter, not gray matter.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to precisely characterize the extent of the defect, confirm the presence of gray matter lining, and evaluate for associated midline anomalies such as septo-optic dysplasia. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to establish the congenital stability of the lesion and to ensure there are no new complications such as evolving hydrocephalus. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for definitively demonstrating whether the cleft is lined by gray or white matter and for identifying subtle associated cortical malformations. I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted sequences to exquisitely differentiate gray from white matter lining the cleft, alongside T2 and FLAIR sequences to evaluate for any surrounding gliosis which would point towards a destructive encephalomalacic etiology. This should go to the Paediatric Neurology MDT. My report would clearly document the presence of the gray matter-lined cleft, whether it is unilateral or bilateral, and the status of the septum pellucidum and optic nerves to guide developmental support and seizure management.
